answer:
**The Falsafa corpus contains no works that reject the binding authority of the past on the present.** Instead, several Dharmaśāstra texts (ancient Indic legal codes attributed to sages) centrally engage this question through explicit discussions of the hierarchy of *dharma* sources.[^1] [^2] [^3] [^4] These rank Vedic revelation (*śruti*) highest, followed by remembered tradition (*smṛti*), the customs/practices of the virtuous/regional past (*ācāra*/*caritra*/*deśadharma*, i.e., binding precedents and ancestral conduct), and finally royal edicts (which may override unreasonable customs but not scripture).[^5] [^6] Custom—constant regional practice, even by the unrighteous—binds as law unless contradicted by higher sources or reason, superseding general rules in some cases.[^7] [^8] Past conduct of the learned virtuous is authoritative, especially in Āryāvarta.[^9]

No other works (e.g., poetry like Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*, Old English elegies, or Comte/Dunoyer's property treatises) systematically address this; searches for related concepts (precedent, ancestors' law, tradition's authority) surface only passing poetic references to clinging to "ancient customs" without analysis.[^10]

### Key Engaging Works
1. **Kātyāyana Smṛti** (Ch. 4): Custom (*caritra*)—regional constant practice—is a source superseding legal procedure (*vyavahāra*) if sovereign-approved; king sets aside unreasonable custom via edict; sacred law (*dharma*) binds first.[^11]
2. **Bṛhaspati Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Four decision means: *dharma*, procedure, custom (local usage/community approval supersedes general rule), king's edict (supersedes custom).[^12]
3. **Manusmṛti** (Ch. 2): *Dharma* sources: Veda, *smṛti*, virtuous conduct (*ācāra*, binding regional tradition), conscience; Brahmavarta customs authoritative.[^13]
4. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Upholds *smṛti*/custom as binding unless contradicted by Veda; adoption/precedents from elders bind.[^14]

Other Smṛtis (e.g., Nārada, Yama, Yājñavalkya, Viṣṇu, Pārāśara) parallel this via shared Dharmaśāstra framework on *ācāra*'s authority, but lack unique chapters scanned here.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 0](works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/00-preface/translation/#p-unknown) (property from past occupation binds).

[^2]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 4, [p-44d48b](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/04/translation/#p-44d48b).

[^3]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-7409b9](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-7409b9).

[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-d70b97](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d70b97).

[^5]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 4, [p-6035ab](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/04/translation/#p-6035ab) to [p-e8d6a5](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/04/translation/#p-e8d6a5).

[^6]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-6bd9fb](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-6bd9fb).

[^7]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 4, [p-7351ad](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/04/translation/#p-7351ad).

[^8]: *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-3e50e7](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-3e50e7).

[^9]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-1627a6](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-1627a6).

[^10]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 1, [p-cce50f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/01-bazm-e-anjum/translation/#p-cce50f).

[^11]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 4, [p-44d48b](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/04/translation/#p-44d48b).

[^12]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-7409b9](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-7409b9).

[^13]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-d70b97](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d70b97).

[^14]: *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-4c73fa](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-4c73fa).
